content.text: Retrieval source provenance for RAG pipelines is the structured recording of which documents, document versions, chunk positions, and index states were used to construct the retrieved context for a given inference call. Unlike general-purpose data lineage, retrieval provenance is specific to the retrieval-augmented generation pattern: it tracks the query, the vector index consulted, the similarity scores of returned chunks, the source document identifiers and versions, and the position within the source document where each chunk originated.

Provenance is attached to each retrieved chunk at retrieval time and aggregated into a retrieval provenance record that is associated with the inference call's lineage identifier. This record makes it possible to answer, for any AI output, not just 'what documents were retrieved' but 'which exact sections, from which versions, with what retrieval confidence, from which index state'.

RAG systems are used to ground AI outputs in verified enterprise knowledge. When an output is incorrect, the first forensic question is whether the error came from the model or from the retrieved context. If the retrieved context itself was incorrect—because it referenced an outdated document version, retrieved the wrong section, or had a low similarity score that should have been filtered—then the fix is in the RAG pipeline, not the model. Provenance records make this determination possible.

For regulated use cases, provenance is also a correctness guarantee. A compliance agent that answers questions about regulatory requirements needs to prove that its answer was grounded in the current version of the relevant regulation, not a version superseded six months ago. Provenance records that include document version identifiers and publication dates provide this proof.
Distinguishes model hallucination from retrieval-sourced errors, directing the fix to the correct component.
Enables AI outputs to include attributable citations (source document, section, version) rather than unsourced claims.
Aggregated provenance data shows which documents are frequently retrieved at low similarity scores, indicating index quality problems.
Provenance records that include document version IDs enable detection of outputs that were generated when outdated document versions were the top retrieval result.
For AI systems providing regulatory guidance, provenance proves which version of a regulation was consulted for each answer.

Full provenance requires tracking chunk IDs, document IDs, document versions, page or section positions, similarity scores, and index versions—significantly more metadata than basic retrieval logging.
Tracking the vector index version at retrieval time requires a mechanism to snapshot or version the index state, which not all vector database platforms support natively.

When source documents are re-chunked (due to document updates or chunking strategy changes), chunk IDs change, making it impossible to directly compare provenance records across the re-chunking boundary.
A RAG pipeline retrieving 10 chunks per query at 100,000 queries per day generates one million chunk provenance records per day. Storage and query infrastructure must be sized accordingly.
A retrieval provenance record captures the complete state of one retrieval call. Multiple provenance records may be associated with a single inference if the agent performs multiple retrieval steps.
Unique identifier for this retrieval call
Foreign key to the inference lineage record
SHA-256 of the query string (never store plaintext for high-tier data)
Identifier of the vector index consulted
Version or snapshot timestamp of the index
List of chunk provenance objects (see below)
Time taken for the retrieval call
Unique identifier of the chunk in the index
Version or last-modified timestamp of the source document
Page number, section heading, or character offset
Cosine similarity between query and chunk embedding
Classification tier of the source document
Hash of chunk content at retrieval time for tamper detection

attrs.alt: RAG retrieval source provenance flow diagram

content.text: Any RAG use case where the accuracy of retrieved sources directly affects the quality of a consequential output.
Agents answering questions about policies, regulations, or procedures need provenance to prove which version of a document was consulted.
Customer-facing outputs that include citations need provenance to generate accurate, attributable references.
Aggregated provenance data is the primary input for detecting index drift, outdated documents, and retrieval quality degradation.

Implement provenance recording as a wrapper around the retrieval call in the RAG pipeline. The wrapper enriches each returned chunk with provenance metadata and writes a provenance record before passing the enriched chunks to the context assembly step.
At document ingestion time, store metadata alongside each chunk in the vector index: source_doc_id, source_doc_version, source_location, and data_tier.
When performing retrieval, request the metadata fields alongside the embedding vectors from the vector database.
Compute the chunk_hash of each returned chunk's content at retrieval time for tamper detection.
Construct a retrieval provenance record and write it asynchronously to the provenance store, linked to the current lineage_id.
Pass the enriched chunks (with provenance metadata attached) to the context assembly step, so the lineage record can reference chunk IDs.
When generating the output, extract the provenance metadata to produce citations or source references in the response.

A financial services firm deploys a regulatory compliance agent that answers analysts' questions about applicable regulations. The agent uses a RAG pipeline over an indexed corpus of regulatory texts, internal policy documents, and legal opinions. Every retrieval is recorded with a provenance record that includes the regulation name, the specific section number retrieved, the publication date of the regulation version indexed, and the similarity score. When an analyst receives an answer, the response includes auto-generated citations in the form 'GDPR Art. 17(1) (indexed: 2024-01-01)'. The firm's compliance team runs a weekly dashboard query over the provenance store, identifying any answers where the retrieved regulation version was more than six months old, flagging them for manual review. When the GDPR implementing regulation was updated, the dashboard identified 23 recent answers that had been grounded in the pre-update text, triggering a re-run of those queries against the updated index.
Not storing document version in the index:
If the vector index does not include a document version field in its metadata, provenance records cannot distinguish between answers grounded in current vs. outdated documents.
Hashing query plaintext in Confidential sessions:
For queries involving Confidential or Restricted data, the query text should be hashed rather than stored in plaintext in the provenance record.
Re-indexing a document corpus without recording a new index version makes it impossible to determine which index state was consulted for a given retrieval.
Chunks retrieved below the similarity threshold but included in context due to fallback logic should still appear in the provenance record to support investigation of low-quality retrievals.
A provenance record that cannot be correlated with the final output (via the lineage_id) cannot support forensic investigation of incorrect answers.
Store provenance metadata at ingestion, not retrieval:
Embedding source_doc_id, version, location, and tier into the vector index metadata at ingestion time means retrieval provenance is available for free without additional lookups.
Use a timestamp or semantic version for each full re-index of the corpus, and record which index version was active at retrieval time.
Filter out chunks below a configurable similarity threshold and record the threshold in the provenance record, so reviewers know the retrieval quality floor that was applied.
Build citation formatters that transform provenance records into standard citation formats (Chicago, APA, or organisation-specific) for inclusion in AI outputs.
Monitor retrieval quality metrics from provenance:
Aggregate provenance records daily to compute metrics: mean similarity score per index, most-retrieved outdated documents, retrieval latency distribution.

The main scalability concern is the volume of provenance records. At ten retrieved chunks per query and 100,000 queries per day, one million chunk provenance records are generated daily. Use columnar storage (Parquet on S3, BigQuery) for long-term provenance storage, and pre-aggregate daily summary tables for dashboard queries. Keep only the retrieval_id, lineage_id, and source_doc_id in a hot query table; store full chunk metadata in cold storage.
Write provenance records to Kafka or Kinesis before writing to the long-term store, decoupling retrieval latency from provenance persistence.
For Restricted-tier retrievals, store only chunk_id and hash in the provenance record; store the full metadata in a separate, access-controlled store.
Vector databases with metadata filtering support; store document version and tier fields alongside embeddings for provenance-enriched retrieval.
LangChain observability platform that captures retrieval provenance automatically for LangChain RAG pipelines.
Open-source RAG evaluation framework that records retrieval provenance and uses it to evaluate faithfulness and answer relevance.
SQL transformation framework for building provenance summary tables and citation quality dashboards over raw provenance records.
Retrieval calls can be traced as spans; chunk-level provenance attached as span attributes integrates with distributed tracing platforms.

Provenance records may indirectly reveal sensitive information if they include document identifiers from Confidential or Restricted sources that are accessible to parties without appropriate clearance. Control access to the provenance store at the same tier as the highest-tier document referenced in the records. For Restricted-tier retrievals, apply the same access controls to provenance records as to the source documents themselves.

The chunk_hash field serves as a tamper-detection mechanism: if a document in the index is modified without triggering a re-index, the hash of the currently indexed chunk will not match the hash recorded at retrieval time, indicating that the provenance record references a version of the chunk that is no longer current.
Retrieval provenance records the source document, version, section, similarity score, and data tier of every chunk included in an AI agent's context via RAG.
Provenance enables error attribution: determining whether an incorrect output came from a bad retrieved source or from model behavior.
Document version tracking in the vector index is a prerequisite for provenance records that can distinguish current from outdated retrievals.
Aggregated provenance data is the primary input for RAG pipeline quality monitoring: mean similarity trends, outdated document detection, and citation accuracy.
Access controls on the provenance store must match the highest classification tier of the documents it references.
